source_paper_title: On Time-Consistent Equilibrium Stopping Under Aggregation of Diverse Discount Rates

Section 5.1 (Example 2 discussion), page 28 (around the paragraph following Remark 5.3).

As a sharp contrast, in our framework with a general attitude function \(\varphi(x)\), it is an appealing problem to find a general characterization of the optimal equilibrium if it exists, which will be left for our future research.
title: Characterize optimal equilibrium stopping regions under general aggregation attitude functions
background_latex:
Consider an infinite-horizon optimal stopping problem for a one-dimensional time-homogeneous diffusion process \(X^x=(X^x_t)_{t\ge 0}\) with initial condition \(X^x_0=x\in\mathbb{R}\), adapted to its natural filtration, on a probability space \((\Omega,\mathcal{F},\mathbb{P})\). Let \(\mathcal{T}\) be the set of all stopping times.

A (possibly random) discount rate \(\rho\) takes values in \([0,\infty)\) with distribution function \(F_\rho\), and is independent of \(X\). Let \(g:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}\) be a payoff function. A strictly increasing function \(\varphi:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}\) (the “attitude function”) aggregates utilities across discount rates.

For a Borel stopping region \(R\subseteq\mathbb{R}\), define the hitting time (starting from state \(x\))
\[\tau(x,R):=\inf\{t>0: X^x_t\in R\}.\]
Define the continuation value under the policy “continue until hitting \(R\)” by
\[J(x,R):=\int_0^\infty \varphi\Big(\mathbb{E}\big[e^{-r\tau(x,R)}\, g(X^x_{\tau(x,R)})\big]\Big)\, dF_\rho(r).\]
Define the immediate-stop payoff as \(\varphi(g(x))\). Define the value induced by a stopping region \(R\) as
\[V(x,R):= \varphi(g(x))\vee J(x,R),\qquad x\in\mathbb{R}.\]

A (mild) time-consistent equilibrium is a Borel set \(R\) that is a fixed point of the policy-improvement operator \(\Theta\) defined by
\[\Theta(R):= S_R\cup R,\qquad S_R:=\{x\notin R: \varphi(g(x))>J(x,R)\}.\]
Let \(\mathcal{E}\) denote the set of all such equilibria.

An “optimal equilibrium” is an equilibrium \(R^*\in\mathcal{E}\) whose induced value dominates that of any other equilibrium pointwise:
\[V(x,R^*)\ge V(x,R)\quad\text{for all }x\in\mathbb{R}\text{ and all }R\in\mathcal{E}.\]

The paper shows that for certain sufficient conditions (and for the Put payoff \(g(x)=(K-x)^+\) with nonnegative diffusions) the smallest equilibrium within a one-barrier class is optimal; however, when these sufficient conditions on \(\varphi\) fail (e.g., \(\varphi(x)=\min\{x,\alpha\}\)), examples show that the optimal equilibrium may differ from the smallest equilibrium or may not exist at all.
problem_definition_latex:
Determine general conditions and/or a general characterization of an optimal equilibrium \(R^*\in\mathcal{E}\) (when it exists) for the time-inconsistent stopping problem with aggregated discount rates, i.e., identify a selection rule or structural description of \(R^*\) such that
\[V(x,R^*)\ge V(x,R)\quad\text{for all }x\in\mathbb{R}\text{ and all }R\in\mathcal{E},\]
in settings where the attitude function \(\varphi\) is strictly increasing but does not satisfy the sufficient condition used in the paper to ensure that the smallest equilibrium is optimal.

\[V(x,R^*)\ge V(x,R)\quad\text{for all }x\in\mathbb{R}\text{ and all }R\in\mathcal{E},\]
in settings where the attitude function \(\varphi\) is strictly increasing but does not satisfy the sufficient condition used in the paper to ensure that the smallest equilibrium is optimal.
location_in_paper: Section 5.1 (Example 2 discussion), page 28 (around the paragraph following Remark 5.3).
open_problem_quote: As a sharp contrast, in our framework with a general attitude function \(\varphi(x)\), it is an appealing problem to find a general characterization of the optimal equilibrium if it exists, which will be left for our future research.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The paper already provides explicit diffusion examples where optimal equilibria may fail to exist, suggesting instance search is possible by varying \(\varphi\) and \(F_\rho\). However, equilibria are defined via fixed points of a set-valued operator on Borel sets, and verifying optimality typically requires solving hitting-time expectations, making large-scale automated counterexample search nontrivial except in parameterized families with closed forms (e.g., geometric Brownian motion).
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Candidate characterizations could be evaluated in specific parametric models by computing \(V(x,R)\) numerically and checking dominance over a discretized family of equilibria, giving a workable automated fitness signal. But the general problem is analytical and model-dependent, and there is no canonical finite representation of arbitrary equilibria beyond restricted families (e.g., one-barrier), limiting fully automated evolutionary discovery.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.
